content:
# Romans Chapter 13 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_013_page_001.jpg

1. Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God.

2. Therefore whoever resists the authorities resists what God has appointed, and those who resist will incur judgment.

3. For rulers are not a terror to good conduct, but to bad. Would you have no fear of the one who is in authority? Then do what is good, and you will receive his approval,

4. for he is God's servant for your good. But if you do wrong, be afraid, for he does not bear the sword in vain. For he is the servant of God, an avenger who carries out God's wrath on the wrongdoer.

5. Therefore one must be in subjection, not only to avoid God's wrath but also for the sake of conscience.

6. For because of this you also pay taxes, for the authorities are ministers of God, attending to this very thing.

7. Pay to all what is owed to them: taxes to whom taxes are owed, revenue to whom revenue is owed, respect to whom respect is owed, honor to whom honor is owed.

8. Owe no one anything, except to love each other, for the one who loves another has fulfilled the law.

9. For the commandments, "You shall not commit adultery, You shall not murder, You shall not steal, You shall not covet," and any other commandment, are summed up in this word: "You shall love your neighbor as yourself."

10. Love does no wrong to a neighbor; therefore love is the fulfilling of the law.

11. Besides this you know the time, that the hour has come for you to wake from sleep. For salvation is nearer to us now than when we first believed.

12. The night is far gone; the day is at hand. So then let us cast off the works of darkness and put on the armor of light.

13. Let us walk properly as in the daytime, not in orgies and drunkenness, not in sexual immorality and sensuality, not in quarreling and jealousy.

14. But put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh, to gratify its desires.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Romans 13 addresses three distinct topics: political submission (vv.1-7), love as law-fulfillment (vv.8-10), and eschatological urgency (vv.11-14). Together they form the practical outworking of the "living sacrifice" imperative of 12:1.

**Verses 1-7 — Governing authorities:** EMET provides the historical context: early Nero, before persecution. LOGOS supplies the essential interpretive framework: Romans 13 and Revelation 13 are complementary — one describes the state functioning properly, the other the state become demonic. PNEUMA calls it "the most abused passage in the Bible." SOPHIA affirms: Paul's teaching is descriptive of the state's ideal function ("God's servant for good"), not an unconditional endorsement of whatever any government does. The passage must be read alongside the prophetic tradition (Daniel 7, Revelation 13) that recognizes the state's capacity for demonic evil. The church's loyalty to the state is conditional on the state functioning as God's servant. When it does not, civil disobedience becomes not just permissible but obligatory (cf. Acts 5:29: "We must obey God rather than human beings").

**Verses 8-10 — Love fulfills the law:** SOPHIA affirms: this passage is the ethical climax of Paul's argument about law and gospel. The law could not produce righteousness (ch.7); the Spirit enables it (8:4); love is its concrete expression (13:8-10). Paul is not abolishing the law — he is identifying its fulfillment. Every specific commandment is an application of the love principle. This echoes Jesus' teaching (Mark 12:28-31) and establishes love as the hermeneutical key to all ethical reasoning.

**Verse 14 — Augustine's conversion verse:** EMET notes the historical connection. SOPHIA affirms: Augustine, in the garden in Milan in 386 CE, heard a voice saying "Take up and read." He opened Paul's letter to Romans and his eyes fell on 13:13-14. The verse that converted one of Christianity's greatest theologians — "Put on the Lord Jesus Christ" — is not merely ethical instruction. It is identity formation. To "put on" Christ is to assume his character as one's own. The baptismal imagery (Galatians 3:27) is present: you clothe yourself in Christ as a garment of new identity.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
